title: President Marcos' ICC options

stances.claim: Marcos has three options on the ICC: (1) maintain the status quo, (2) rejoin the ICC, or (3) cooperate without rejoining.
stances.rhetorical_move: structured enumeration of presidential options as advisory framing

stances.claim: The ICC's Appeals Chamber majority skirted the fundamental jurisdictional issue (the two-year reglementary period); the vocal minority of two was correct that the ICC did not acquire jurisdiction because both the petition and the PTC grant came more than two years after the Philippine withdrawal took effect on March 17, 2019.
stances.rhetorical_move: doctrinal alignment with minority view + procedural-time-bar argument

stances.claim: Because the Duterte withdrawal was made unilaterally by the President without Senate concurrence, any President can simply reverse it by recalling the letter of withdrawal — Senate Resolution No. 57's ratification of the Rome Statute (August 23, 2011) still 'mightily stands.'
stances.rhetorical_move: constitutional executive-power analysis grounded in foreign-affairs prerogative

stances.claim: Under the third option, opening NBI, PNP, and Chief Prosecutor facilities to Khan can yield two outcomes: (a) ICC investigators conclude Philippine institutions are genuinely willing and able, or (b) investigators rely on their own findings and 'ensnare the alleged perpetrators into the sticky ICC web.'
stances.rhetorical_move: two-outcome scenario analysis + metaphor ('sticky ICC web')

events: House of Representatives hearings on ICC cooperation

decision_framework_signals: structured options-analysis framework (status quo / rejoin / cooperate without rejoining) as presidential advisory device
applies two-year-reglementary-period procedural argument as jurisdictional defense
uses executive-foreign-affairs-prerogative doctrine to argue presidential reversibility of unilateral acts
